quiz_questions.question_text: An observational study:

options.text: Imposes treatments on subjects
Observes without intervention
Always establishes causation
Requires blinding of all participants

quiz_questions.explanation: Observational studies collect data without manipulating any variables, so they cannot establish causation on their own.

quiz_questions.question_text: Only experiments can establish:

options.text: Association
Correlation
Cause and effect
Observational results

quiz_questions.explanation: Random assignment and treatment control in experiments allow researchers to draw causal conclusions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Random assignment is used to:

options.text: Select the sample from the population
Increase sample size
Balance confounding variables across treatment groups
Eliminate all bias entirely

quiz_questions.explanation: Random assignment distributes both known and unknown confounders approximately equally between groups.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a double-blind experiment:

options.text: Only the subject is blind to the treatment
Only the researcher is blind to the treatment
Neither subject nor researcher knows group assignments
Everyone knows their group assignments

quiz_questions.explanation: Double-blind prevents both subject and researcher bias by keeping group assignments hidden from both parties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Replication in experiments means:

options.text: Repeating the entire study at a different university
Using enough subjects for reliable results
Copying another researcher's data
Having exactly one subject per group

quiz_questions.explanation: Sufficient replication (enough subjects) increases the reliability and statistical power of experimental results.

quiz_questions.question_text: A placebo is:

options.text: An effective drug given to all participants
An inactive treatment given to the control group
A measurement tool for recording outcomes
A type of systematic bias

quiz_questions.explanation: Placebos allow comparison by mimicking the treatment experience without the active ingredient.

quiz_questions.question_text: Random sampling selects from the population; random assignment:

options.text: Does the same thing as random sampling
Assigns subjects to treatment groups
Is unnecessary in well-designed experiments
Selects clusters from the population

quiz_questions.explanation: Random sampling determines who is studied, while random assignment determines who receives which treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a matched-pairs design:

options.text: Two completely separate groups are compared
Each subject serves as their own control or is paired with a similar subject
Clusters of subjects are randomly chosen
No control group exists in the design

quiz_questions.explanation: Matching ensures pairs are similar on key characteristics, reducing the effect of confounding variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: A randomized block design:

options.text: Groups similar subjects into blocks, then randomly assigns treatments within blocks
Ignores blocking factors entirely
Uses no randomization at all
Is the same as cluster sampling

quiz_questions.explanation: Blocking reduces variability by grouping similar subjects together before randomly assigning treatments within each block.

quiz_questions.question_text: An experiment with no control group:

options.text: Is perfectly valid for establishing causation
Cannot determine whether the treatment caused the observed effect
Is better than a controlled experiment
Is the most common experimental design

quiz_questions.explanation: Without a control group, there is no baseline for comparison, making it impossible to attribute changes to the treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a confounding variable?

options.text: A variable that is always controlled in an experiment
A variable that affects both the explanatory and response variables, distorting the relationship
The dependent variable being measured
A variable that improves the accuracy of an experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: A confounding variable is associated with both the explanatory and response variables, making it difficult to determine which variable is truly responsible for the observed effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the placebo effect refer to?

options.text: A measurement error caused by faulty instruments
An improvement caused by the subject's belief they are receiving treatment
A type of sampling method used in surveys
The effect of increasing sample size on results

quiz_questions.explanation: The placebo effect occurs when subjects experience real improvements simply because they believe they are receiving an active treatment, even when given an inert substance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are lurking variables?

options.text: Variables that are always measured and reported
The treatment variables in an experiment
Unmeasured variables that may affect the results of a study
Variables that only exist in experimental studies

quiz_questions.explanation: Lurking variables are confounders that were not measured or controlled in the study but may still influence the observed relationship between the variables of interest.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the response variable in an experiment?

options.text: The variable imposed by the researcher
The confounding variable
The outcome being measured to assess the effect of the treatment
The sample size of the experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: The response variable (dependent variable) is the outcome measured to determine whether the explanatory variable had an effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'explanatory variable' mean?

options.text: The outcome of the experiment
The variable believed to cause or explain changes in the response variable
A lurking variable that was not measured
The total number of subjects in the study

quiz_questions.explanation: The explanatory variable (independent variable or treatment) is the factor the researcher manipulates to observe its effect on the response variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'single-blind' mean in experimental design?

options.text: The subject does not know which treatment group they are in
The researcher does not know which group each subject belongs to
Everyone in the study knows all group assignments
Nobody in the study knows the purpose of the research

quiz_questions.explanation: In a single-blind experiment, subjects are kept unaware of their group assignment to prevent their expectations from influencing the results.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'informed consent' mean in research?

options.text: Subjects are told nothing about the study beforehand
Only researchers are informed about the study's details
Subjects understand and voluntarily agree to participate, knowing the study details and risks
Consent is optional and not required for any study

quiz_questions.explanation: Informed consent is an ethical requirement ensuring that participants voluntarily agree to take part after being fully informed about the study's purpose, procedures, and potential risks.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'blinding' in an experiment?

options.text: Reducing the cost of the experiment
Preventing subjects, researchers, or both from knowing treatment assignments to reduce bias
Increasing the number of subjects in the study
Completely eliminating all confounding variables

quiz_questions.explanation: Blinding prevents expectations from influencing the results by hiding group assignments from subjects, researchers, or both.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a treatment group?

options.text: The group that receives the drug or intervention being tested
The group that receives a placebo
The group that receives no treatment at all
The group that designs the experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: The treatment group receives the active drug, intervention, or condition being tested in the experiment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'statistical significance'?

options.text: The result is practically important in everyday life
The result is unlikely to have occurred by chance alone
The sample is extremely large
There is absolutely no error in the measurements

quiz_questions.explanation: Statistical significance indicates that the observed effect is large enough that it is unlikely to be due to random chance, typically evaluated using a p-value threshold.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company wants to test whether a new migraine medication reduces headache duration. They recruit 200 migraine sufferers and randomly assign 100 to receive the medication and 100 to receive a sugar pill. Neither the patients nor the doctors know who receives which pill. After 8 weeks, the average headache duration is 2.1 hours shorter in the medication group. What type of study design is this?

options.text: An observational study
A double-blind randomized controlled experiment
A case-control study
A voluntary response survey

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a double-blind randomized controlled experiment because subjects are randomly assigned to treatment and control groups, a placebo is used, and neither patients nor doctors know the assignments.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher notices that students who eat breakfast tend to have higher GPAs than those who skip breakfast. She concludes that eating breakfast causes better academic performance. What is the main flaw in this reasoning?

options.text: The sample size is too small
This is an observational study, so confounding variables (like socioeconomic status) may explain the association
She should have used a larger margin of error
The study used too much blinding

quiz_questions.explanation: Without random assignment, confounding variables such as household income, parental involvement, or sleep habits could explain the observed association between breakfast and GPA.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school principal wants to test whether a new after-school tutoring program improves math scores. She has 60 students volunteer and lets them choose whether to attend tutoring or not. Students who attend tutoring score 15 points higher on average. Can she conclude the tutoring caused the improvement?

options.text: Yes, because the difference of 15 points is large
Yes, because she had 60 students in the study
No, because students self-selected into groups, introducing confounding variables like motivation
No, because 60 students is too few for any study

quiz_questions.explanation: Self-selection means more motivated students may have chosen tutoring, and their motivation (not the tutoring itself) could explain the higher scores. Random assignment would be needed for a causal claim.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cosmetics company tests a new anti-aging cream. Group A applies the cream daily, and Group B applies a moisturizer that looks and feels identical but has no active ingredients. Researchers measuring skin elasticity do not know which group each participant belongs to. After 12 weeks, Group A shows a 20% improvement. What role does the identical-looking moisturizer serve?

options.text: It serves as the explanatory variable
It serves as the placebo to control for the placebo effect
It serves as a confounding variable
It serves as the blocking factor

quiz_questions.explanation: The identical-looking moisturizer without active ingredients is a placebo, ensuring that any measured improvements can be attributed to the active cream rather than the psychological effect of applying a product.

quiz_questions.question_text: An agricultural researcher wants to test three fertilizer types on crop yield. The farm has sections with different soil types (sandy, clay, loamy). She divides each soil section into three plots and randomly assigns one fertilizer to each plot within each section. What experimental design is she using?

options.text: A completely randomized design
A matched-pairs design
A randomized block design with soil type as the blocking factor
An observational study

quiz_questions.explanation: By grouping plots by soil type (blocks) and randomly assigning fertilizers within each block, the researcher uses a randomized block design to control for the confounding effect of soil differences.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fitness company claims their new protein shake builds muscle faster. They recruit 40 gym members and randomly assign 20 to drink the shake daily and 20 to drink a similar-tasting placebo shake. After 3 months, the protein shake group gains an average of 3.2 pounds of muscle while the placebo group gains 2.9 pounds. The difference is not statistically significant (p = 0.42). What should you conclude?

options.text: The protein shake definitively does not work
There is not enough evidence to conclude the shake produces greater muscle gain than the placebo
The study proves the shake is harmful
The 0.3-pound difference proves the shake works

quiz_questions.explanation: A p-value of 0.42 means the observed difference could easily have occurred by chance. We fail to reject the null hypothesis, meaning there is insufficient evidence that the shake is more effective than the placebo.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sleep researcher wants to compare two types of white noise machines on sleep quality. She pairs 30 participants by age and baseline sleep scores, creating 15 pairs. Within each pair, one person is randomly assigned Machine A and the other Machine B. What design is this?

options.text: A completely randomized design
A matched-pairs design
A single-blind observational study
A stratified random sampling plan

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a matched-pairs design because participants are paired based on similar characteristics (age and sleep scores), and treatments are randomly assigned within each pair to control for those characteristics.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city health department surveys 5,000 residents and finds that people who live near parks exercise more and have lower rates of heart disease. A newspaper headline reads: 'Living near parks prevents heart disease.' What is wrong with this headline?

options.text: The sample size is too small for any conclusion
The study is observational, so it cannot establish that park proximity causes lower heart disease rates
The margin of error is too large
Parks have nothing to do with health outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is an observational study, so confounders such as income, lifestyle, and access to healthcare could explain the association. Only a randomized experiment could support a causal claim.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher wants to test whether background music helps students concentrate during exams. She teaches two sections of the same course. In Section A, she plays classical music during exams; in Section B, there is silence. Section A scores 5 points higher on average. A classmate argues this is not a valid experiment. Why might the classmate be correct?

options.text: The sample size is too small
Students were not randomly assigned to sections, so pre-existing differences between sections could explain the result
Classical music has been proven to always help concentration
Five points is too large a difference to be meaningful

quiz_questions.explanation: Without random assignment of students to sections, the groups may differ in ability, motivation, or other factors, making it impossible to attribute the score difference solely to the music.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital conducts a study on a new blood pressure medication. They enroll 300 patients with high blood pressure. Patients are randomly assigned to receive either the new medication, the current standard medication, or a placebo. After 6 months, the new medication group has the lowest average blood pressure. How many groups are in this experiment, and what type of control groups are used?

options.text: 2 groups: one treatment and one control
3 groups: new medication (treatment), standard medication (active control), and placebo (placebo control)
4 groups: two treatments and two controls
1 group: all patients receive the new medication

quiz_questions.explanation: There are 3 groups: the new medication is the treatment, the standard medication serves as an active control, and the placebo serves as a placebo control, allowing comparison against both no treatment and existing treatment.
